title: Medicare 8-Minute Rule Billing Guidelines (2026): Your Essential Guide to Therapy Units
rawContent:
Understanding the Medicare 8-Minute Rule for 2026

Eight minutes. That small number determines whether a therapy service gets reimbursed, or not. The Medicare 8-Minute Rule is a Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) regulation that governs how timed therapy services are counted and billed under Medicare Part B.

The rule creates a standardized method for calculating billable units across physical therapy, occupational therapy, and speech-language pathology services. Without it, billing for time-based services would be inconsistent and open to significant error.

What this means for you: Your therapist uses this rule to make sure Medicare is billed correctly for the time spent on your care.

Its purpose is straightforward: ensure that reimbursement accurately reflects the actual time a therapist spends delivering direct treatment. For therapy providers, getting this right is not optional. Accurate unit calculation protects revenue, supports compliance, and keeps claims from being flagged for audit.

As of 2026, the rule remains fully in effect and is consistent with CMS Transmittal 1780. Therapy providers operating under Medicare Part B must continue applying its methodology to every timed service they bill.

Who Needs to Follow the Medicare 8-Minute Rule?

The rule applies directly to any provider billing timed therapy services under Medicare Part B. That includes Physical Therapists (PTs), Occupational Therapists (OTs), Speech-Language Pathologists (SLPs), and their assistants, Physical Therapist Assistants (PTAs) and Occupational Therapist Assistants (OTAs).

The settings where this rule applies are broad. Private practices, hospital outpatient departments, skilled nursing facilities, and home health agencies all fall under its requirements. If Medicare Part B is the payer, the 8-Minute Rule is in play.

Certified Athletic Trainers may also be subject to the rule when billing for services provided under physician supervision in certain clinical settings. The key factor is always whether Medicare Part B is being billed, and whether the service uses a timed CPT code.

Timed vs. Untimed Services: What the 8-Minute Rule Covers

Not every therapy service falls under the 8-Minute Rule. The rule applies only to timed CPT codes procedure codes where treatment time is a direct component of what's being delivered.

Common examples of timed codes include:

  • 97110 Therapeutic Exercise

  • 97140 Manual Therapy Techniques

  • 97035 Ultrasound

  • 97530 Therapeutic Activities

  • 97112 Neuromuscular Reeducation

On the other side are untimed (service-based) codes. These are billed once per session regardless of how long the service takes. Examples include initial evaluations (97161-97163) and orthotic fittings. Time spent on untimed services does not count toward the 8-minute threshold calculation.

This distinction matters because mixing up the two categories is one of the most common billing errors in therapy practices. Only time spent on timed procedures feeds into the unit calculation process.

Calculating Therapy Units: The Core of the 8-Minute Rule

Each billable unit equals 15 minutes of treatment time. The 8-Minute Rule determines the minimum time needed to bill even one unit, and it sets the thresholds for billing additional units as total timed minutes increase.

Here is the standard unit conversion table:

Total Timed Minutes

Billable Units

8 – 22 minutes

1 unit

23 – 37 minutes

2 units

38 – 52 minutes

3 units

53 – 67 minutes

4 units

68 – 82 minutes

5 units

83 – 97 minutes

6 units

98 – 112 minutes

7 units

113 – 127 minutes

8 units

The most important step is to sum the total timed minutes first, then apply the table. Do not calculate units for each procedure separately, that is a known billing error.

Here's a step-by-step example of how to calculate units:

  1. Therapeutic Exercise (97110): 25 minutes

  2. Manual Therapy (97140): 15 minutes

  3. Neuromuscular Reeducation (97112): 10 minutes

  4. Total timed minutes: 50 minutes

  5. 50 minutes falls in the 38–52 minute range = 3 billable units

Those 3 units are then allocated across the procedures, typically assigning more units to the service that consumed the most time. Therapeutic Exercise would receive 2 units (for 25 minutes), and Manual Therapy or Neuromuscular Reeducation would each receive 1 unit, depending on which had the greater remaining time.

For patients recovering from stroke or neurological conditions, understanding how occupational therapy services are covered can help set realistic expectations for how sessions are billed.

Essential Billing Modifiers for Medicare Therapy Claims

Every timed therapy claim submitted to Medicare requires a discipline-specific modifier. These modifiers tell Medicare which type of therapist provided the service and are mandatory, not optional.

  • GP Services delivered under a Physical Therapy plan of care

  • GO Services delivered under an Occupational Therapy plan of care

  • GN Services delivered under a Speech-Language Pathology plan of care

These modifiers must accompany all timed CPT codes on every Medicare Part B claim. Missing or incorrect modifiers are among the most frequent reasons therapy claims are denied or delayed in processing.

Beyond claim accuracy, the modifiers serve a tracking function. Medicare uses them to monitor therapy utilization by discipline, which feeds directly into the targeted medical review process. Getting the modifier right the first time protects your claims and supports accurate records under the Medicare Physician Fee Schedule.

Navigating Therapy Spending Limits and Targeted Medical Review in 2026

The hard therapy caps that once restricted annual Medicare therapy spending were permanently eliminated by the Bipartisan Budget Act of 2018. However, the targeted medical review process that replaced them is still active in 2026.

The 2026 threshold for targeted medical review is $3,000 for combined Physical Therapy and Speech-Language Pathology services, and a separate $3,000 threshold for Occupational Therapy. Once a patient's claims exceed these amounts in a calendar year, Medicare flags those claims for closer scrutiny.

This is where precise 8-Minute Rule billing becomes especially important. Over-billed units, even unintentional ones, can push a patient over the threshold prematurely. That draws attention to all claims, not just the inflated ones.

Proper documentation and accurate unit calculation are your strongest defenses. When services above the threshold are medically necessary, understanding medical necessity standards helps therapists build the right justification from the start.

Compliance and Consequences: Avoiding 8-Minute Rule Violations

Billing under the 8-Minute Rule without adequate documentation is one of the fastest ways to trigger a claim denial or audit. Medicare requires detailed, contemporaneous records to support every unit billed.

Documentation must include:

  • Start and stop times for each timed procedure

  • Progress notes describing treatment and patient response

  • Therapy logs showing total time per session

  • Evidence that services were medically necessary

  • The plan of care, signed and dated by the supervising physician or therapist

When documentation does not support the units billed, Medicare can deny the claim outright or require repayment of funds already received. Recovery Audit Contractors (RACs) actively review therapy claims and have authority to recoup payments going back multiple years.

Corrective actions start with a thorough internal audit. Review your billing for common errors: incorrect unit counts, missing modifiers, or time calculations that don't match the treatment notes. If errors are found, proactively reporting and correcting them through the appropriate Medicare channels reduces exposure significantly.

Staying familiar with Medicare rights and protections also helps providers and patients alike understand what recourse exists if claims are incorrectly denied.

Pro Tip

Before submitting any therapy claim, cross-reference your total timed minutes in the treatment note against the units billed on the claim form, every single session. A five-minute documentation discrepancy can flip a 3-unit claim down to 2 units, and doing this check in real time costs far less than responding to a RAC audit months later.

Beyond the 8-Minute Rule: Payment Reductions and Other Considerations

Accurate unit calculation is essential, but it is not the only factor that shapes therapy reimbursement under Medicare Part B. The Multiple Procedure Payment Reduction (MPPR) policy also applies when a therapist bills more than one timed procedure in the same session.

Under MPPR, the highest-valued procedure in the session is reimbursed at 100% of the Medicare Physician Fee Schedule rate. Each subsequent timed procedure in the same session is reimbursed at only 75%. This applies to physical therapy, occupational therapy, and speech-language pathology services delivered on the same day by the same provider.

Here's a summary of the MPPR payment reduction rates:

Procedure Order

Payment Reduction

Highest-valued procedure

100% of fee schedule

All subsequent timed procedures

75% of fee schedule

Patients also share in the cost of therapy services. The 2026 Medicare Part B deductible is $283, and after that, the standard 20% coinsurance applies. For patients managing these out-of-pocket costs, Medicare cost-sharing rules explain how these amounts are structured.

Supplemental coverage can reduce patient liability significantly. Medigap plans cover some or all of the Part B coinsurance, depending on the plan type. Medicare Advantage plans handle therapy cost-sharing differently, often through fixed copays rather than percentage-based coinsurance.

CMS updates its billing guidelines periodically. Staying current with transmittals and fee schedule updates is not a one-time task, it is an ongoing responsibility for every therapy provider billing Medicare.

Frequently Asked Questions About the Medicare 8-Minute Rule (2026)

Does the Medicare 8-minute rule apply to all therapy services in 2026?

No. The rule applies only to timed CPT codes, where treatment time is a direct component of the service. Untimed (service-based) codes, such as evaluations, are billed once per session regardless of how long they take and are not subject to the 8-minute calculation.

What CPT codes are subject to the Medicare 8-minute rule?

Common timed codes include 97110 (Therapeutic Exercise), 97140 (Manual Therapy), 97112 (Neuromuscular Reeducation), 97530 (Therapeutic Activities), and 97035 (Ultrasound). CMS publishes the full list of timed codes in its therapy billing guidance, and providers should verify current codes annually.

How does the 8-minute rule affect billing for multiple therapy services in one session?

Total timed minutes across all timed procedures must be added together first. That combined total is then applied to the unit conversion table to determine the total billable units. Units are allocated across the individual procedures based on the time spent on each.

Are there any exceptions to the Medicare 8-minute rule?

The rule applies consistently across Medicare Part B settings, with very limited exception. Some Medicare Advantage plans may have their own billing policies that differ slightly, so providers should verify requirements with individual plans. The core 8-minute standard itself, however, applies broadly across Part B therapy billing.

What documentation is required to support billing under the Medicare 8-minute rule?

You must document the start and stop time for each timed procedure, a description of the treatment and patient response, evidence of medical necessity, and a signed plan of care. Vague or incomplete notes that don't match the units billed are the primary trigger for claim denials and audits.

What happens if I incorrectly bill using the Medicare 8-minute rule?

Medicare can deny the claim, require repayment of previously received funds, or refer the case to a Recovery Audit Contractor for further review. Repeated or systematic errors may escalate to a compliance investigation. Promptly correcting errors and maintaining clean internal records reduces risk considerably.

Mastering Medicare 8-Minute Rule Compliance

The Medicare 8-Minute Rule is one of the most precise billing standards in the Medicare system, and for good reason. Time-based therapy services require a consistent, auditable method for translating treatment time into reimbursable units.

For 2026, the core principles remain unchanged. Sum all timed minutes before calculating units. Apply the correct discipline modifier on every claim. Document start and stop times for each procedure, every session.

Beyond the mechanics, accurate compliance protects your practice. It reduces audit exposure, supports the medical necessity of your services, and ensures patients receive the full benefit of their Medicare Part B coverage without billing irregularities affecting their care.

CMS guidelines do evolve. Staying current with annual fee schedule updates, transmittal changes, and targeted medical review thresholds is part of responsible billing practice. If you have questions about how Medicare covers specific therapy services or want to review your current coverage, speaking with a licensed Medicare professional is a practical next step. You can also explore Medicare coverage for physical therapy to better understand what services are available and how they are reimbursed.
excerpt: The Medicare 8-Minute Rule, a CMS regulation, dictates how timed therapy services are billed under Medicare Part B, ensuring accurate reimbursement for direct treatment time.
author: David Haass
keyTakeaways: The Medicare 8-Minute Rule applies to timed therapy services billed under Medicare Part B.
It standardizes billing for physical therapy, occupational therapy, and speech-language pathology.
Providers must accurately calculate units to ensure correct reimbursement and compliance.
The rule remains in effect for 2026 and applies to various settings and providers.
Only timed CPT codes are subject to the 8-Minute Rule, not all therapy services.
